title: Chunking
text: Chunking splits source documents into smaller passages before embedding. Smaller chunks improve retrieval precision because each vector represents a focused span of text, but chunks that are too small lose surrounding context. A common strategy uses 200 to 500 token chunks with a 10 to 20 percent overlap so that a sentence split across a boundary still appears whole in at least one chunk.

title: Embeddings
text: An embedding model maps text to a fixed-length vector of floating point numbers so that semantically similar passages land close together in vector space. Retrieval compares the query embedding against stored chunk embeddings using a distance metric. Because two different embedding models produce incompatible vector spaces, the query and the stored chunks must be embedded with the same model.

title: Reranking
text: A reranker is a second-stage model that re-scores the top candidates returned by the initial vector search. Vector search optimizes for recall and speed, so it may surface loosely related passages. A cross-encoder reranker reads the query and each candidate together and assigns a sharper relevance score, improving precision at the cost of extra latency.

title: Hybrid retrieval
text: Hybrid retrieval combines dense vector search with sparse keyword search such as BM25. Dense search captures meaning even when wording differs, while keyword search reliably matches exact terms like error codes, product names, or identifiers. Fusing both result sets, often with reciprocal rank fusion, recovers documents that either method alone would miss.

title: Context window
text: The context window is the maximum number of tokens a language model can attend to in a single request, covering both the retrieved passages and the generated answer. When retrieved context exceeds the window the system must truncate or further rank passages. Stuffing too many low-relevance chunks can bury the useful passage and degrade answer quality, an effect sometimes called context dilution.

title: pgvector overview
text: pgvector is a PostgreSQL extension that adds a vector column type and vector similarity operators, letting a relational database store and search embeddings without a separate vector store. It supports exact nearest-neighbor search by default and approximate search through optional indexes.

title: HNSW index
text: The HNSW index in pgvector builds a multi-layer proximity graph for approximate nearest-neighbor search. It offers high recall and fast queries and, unlike IVFFlat, can be created on an empty table because it does not need to learn cluster centroids from existing data. Its build time and memory use are higher than IVFFlat.

title: IVFFlat index
text: The IVFFlat index partitions vectors into lists around centroids learned by k-means, then searches only the nearest lists at query time. It must be built after the table already holds representative data, and its recall depends on the number of lists and the probes parameter chosen at query time.

title: Distance operators
text: pgvector exposes operators for several distance metrics: the L2 distance operator for Euclidean distance, an inner product operator, and a cosine distance operator. The chosen index must match the operator used in queries, and the metric should match how the embedding model was trained, which for most modern text models is cosine similarity.

title: Vector dimensions
text: A pgvector column declares a fixed dimensionality, such as vector with 768 components, and every stored vector must have exactly that many components. Storing embeddings from a model with a different output dimension requires either reconfiguring the column or projecting the vectors, so the embedding dimension is fixed early in a deployment.

title: 2xx success codes
text: HTTP status codes in the 2xx range indicate success. 200 OK is the standard response for a successful GET. 201 Created signals that a request succeeded and a new resource was created, typically returning its location. 204 No Content means the request succeeded but there is no body to return.

title: 4xx client errors
text: HTTP status codes in the 4xx range indicate client errors. 400 Bad Request means the server could not parse the request. 401 Unauthorized means authentication is required or has failed, while 403 Forbidden means the client is authenticated but not allowed. 404 Not Found means the resource does not exist.

title: HTTP methods
text: HTTP defines methods with different semantics. GET retrieves a resource and must be safe and idempotent. POST submits data and is neither safe nor idempotent. PUT replaces a resource and is idempotent. DELETE removes a resource. Idempotent means that repeating the request has the same effect as making it once.

title: Cache-Control
text: The Cache-Control header governs HTTP caching. The max-age directive sets how many seconds a response stays fresh. The stale-while-revalidate directive lets a cache serve a stale response while it fetches a fresh one in the background. The no-store directive forbids any caching, which suits sensitive or highly dynamic responses.

title: CORS
text: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing uses response headers to tell a browser which origins may read a response. The Access-Control-Allow-Origin header names the permitted origin or a wildcard. Browsers send a preflight OPTIONS request for non-simple requests, and the server must answer it before the actual request proceeds.
